summary:
The death of Alexander the Great (June 10–11, 323 BC) in Babylon, at the age of thirty-two, abruptly ended both the personal reign of history's most successful conqueror and the unified empire he had created in less than thirteen years of campaigning. The cause of death has never been definitively established, and the debate continues among historians and medical scholars.

Alexander fell ill after a night of heavy drinking at a banquet in Babylon and died eleven or twelve days later, having been unable to speak in his final days. Ancient sources report the disease progressed through fever, increasing weakness, and eventual paralysis of voice and limbs. The range of proposed diagnoses is broad: typhoid fever complicated by perforation and ascending paralysis, West Nile virus, poisoning, and alcoholic liver disease, among others. The late ancient tradition of poisoning, allegedly organized by Antipater and involving Aristotle's nephew Cassander, is now largely discounted by scholars but never fully disappears from discussion.

On his deathbed, when asked to whom he left his empire, Alexander reportedly said 'to the strongest' — or, in another version, 'to the best.' He may have intended to bequeath a ring to Perdiccas as regent for his unborn child, but the succession was fatally ambiguous. His senior generals immediately began maneuvering for power, triggering the [[event:1039|Wars of the Diadochi]] that would divide and re-divide his empire for decades. The greatest personal empire in antiquity did not survive its creator by even a single generation intact.

Alexander's death at thirty-two has been a source of endless historical speculation: what might he have accomplished with another twenty or thirty years? His plans reportedly included an Arabian campaign, a circumnavigation of Africa, and campaigns in the western Mediterranean. The world had not seen his like, and would not again.
